summary:
The Mongol conquest of the Southern Song Dynasty (1235–1279 AD) was the final phase of the [[event:2226|Mongol conquest of China]], in which [[event:2229|Kublai Khan]] completed the destruction of the last surviving Chinese empire and established the [[event:2980|Yuan Dynasty]] over a unified China for the first time in over three centuries. The Southern Song, which had survived the Mongol conquest of northern China (the Jin Dynasty, 1234) and maintained its prosperous civilization in the Yangtze River basin and south, proved far more difficult to defeat than the northern states: the terrain (rivers, paddy fields, dense cities) favored defense, the Song navy was formidable, and the population was large and wealthy enough to sustain prolonged resistance.

The conquest required the Mongols to develop naval warfare on an unprecedented scale — Kublai recruited Chinese and Korean shipbuilders and naval officers to create fleets capable of amphibious operations. The siege of Xiangyang (1267–1273) was a six-year operation that demonstrated the extraordinary logistical scale of the campaign: the Song garrison held the city on the Han River for six years until Persian engineers from the Ilkhanate brought counterweight trebuchets (the 'Muslim machines') that broke the siege. The fall of Xiangyang opened the Yangtze to Mongol advance; the last Song resistance ended at the naval Battle of Yamen (1279), where the last Song emperor — a seven-year-old child — was carried into the sea by a loyal minister and drowned rather than submit to Mongol capture. The completeness of the Mongol victory over China made subsequent Ming Dynasty nationalism all the more fervent in its anti-Mongol sentiment.
